content.text: Capability Gap Closure and Progression Plans

Knowing where your enterprise stands on the Claude maturity model is only half the work. The critical second half is designing an actionable plan to close the identified gaps and progress across maturity dimensions in a sequence that maximises value while managing risk and organisational change capacity.

This document provides a structured methodology for translating maturity assessment findings into a multi-horizon progression roadmap, prioritising gaps by business impact and closure effort, and embedding the tracking mechanisms that keep programmes on course.

attrs.alt: Three-horizon capability gap closure roadmap showing foundational, scaled, and strategic progression stages

content.text: From Assessment to Action: The Gap-Closure Sequence
Raw maturity scores produce a heat-map of deficiencies. Converting that heat-map into a sequenced programme requires four decisions made in order:

Some capability gaps prevent progress on everything else. If your data-classification guardrails do not exist, no governed use case can be approved. If basic usage monitoring is absent, no ROI claim can be defended. These blockers must be addressed first, regardless of business impact.
Which gaps have the best impact-to-effort ratio?
Among non-blocking gaps, prioritise those where modest investment yields disproportionate lift — typically foundational tooling shared across many use cases.
What change capacity does the organisation have per quarter?
Every programme competes for the same engineering, change-management, and budget bandwidth. Overloading a quarter creates false progress and burnout.

A prompt-ops pipeline (H2) cannot be built without a prompt-engineering competency (H1). Model evaluation harnesses need test-case libraries that take time to curate. Respect dependency order or you waste effort.

A common failure mode: organisations fix gaps in isolated workstreams, each optimised for its own KPIs, without a shared dependency graph. The result is a platform without prompts, or a CoE without a platform to govern.
Capability gap closure should be phased across three overlapping horizons that balance quick wins, scaling infrastructure, and strategic differentiation:

Horizon 1 is about establishing the minimum viable governance, safety, and skill infrastructure that makes Claude use safe to expand. Organisations often want to skip this phase in pursuit of impact, but every H2 programme that skips H1 eventually backtracks.
Structured training for 80% of active users
Real-time API usage + cost by team
Formal intake, review, and sign-off process
Horizon 2 moves from individual experimentation to programmatic delivery. The signature investment is infrastructure that makes Claude capabilities reusable and governable at scale:

A curated internal API layer that wraps Claude with organisation-specific defaults — system prompts, guardrails, model selection, and cost attribution. Engineering teams build once; others consume safely.

Version-controlled prompt libraries, A/B evaluation against golden test sets, CI gates that block prompt regressions, and automated deployment. Treats prompts as code artefacts with the same discipline as software.

A cross-functional team that owns the platform, curates the prompt library, qualifies new use cases, and disseminates best practices. CoE is the difference between a programme and a collection of projects.

Domain-specific benchmark suites (human preference, factual accuracy, task-completion rate) run automatically on every model update and prompt change. Catches regressions before they reach production.
Standardised benefit-measurement templates applied across all live use cases, consolidated monthly. Required for executive reporting and budget defence.
Horizon 3 builds capabilities that are hard to replicate because they compound over time with proprietary data, learned feedback signals, and embedded market relationships:
RLHF-adjacent processes where human corrections to Claude outputs are captured, curated, and used to fine-tune domain-specific models or update prompt libraries systematically.

Products and services that are fundamentally impossible without AI at their core — not AI-enhanced existing products. Requires deep integration of Claude capabilities into the product roadmap and go-to-market.
Vendor, reseller, or API-partner relationships that extend Claude capabilities beyond the enterprise boundary — customer-facing agents, third-party integrations, or white-labelled AI services.
Historical usage data and ML forecasting that predicts compute demand, flags impending capability bottlenecks, and triggers procurement before shortfalls affect delivery.
Not all gaps deserve equal urgency. Use the two-axis prioritisation matrix below to place each identified gap and determine its treatment:

attrs.alt: Gap prioritisation matrix with quadrants for quick wins, priority gaps, strategic investments, and deferred items

content.text: High business value, achievable within one or two sprints with available skills
Address immediately in H1–H2; assign dedicated owner and firm deadline
Small incremental gains, minimal investment required
Batch and execute opportunistically; do not crowd out priority gaps
Transformative but requires sustained multi-quarter investment
Sequence into H2–H3; secure long-term executive sponsorship
Large resource demand with marginal return
Formal defer decision; revisit annually as context changes
Each identified capability gap should have a one-page closure plan with these six fields:

# Gap Closure Plan: [Dimension] — [Gap Name]

## Current State
Maturity level: L[n]
Evidence: [what the assessment observed — e.g., 'no prompt versioning, engineers fork
files manually']

## Target State
Maturity level: L[n+1] or L[n+2]
Success criteria: [measurable, observable — e.g., 'all production prompts stored in
git, CI gate blocks unreviewed changes']

## Horizon
H[1|2|3] — Target completion: [quarter]

## Closure Activities
1. [Action 1] — Owner: [role] — Due: [date]
2. [Action 2] — Owner: [role] — Due: [date]
3. [Action 3] — Owner: [role] — Due: [date]

## Dependencies
- Requires: [prerequisite gaps or programmes]
- Enables: [downstream gaps that unblock]

## Investment Required
Engineering effort: [person-weeks]
Tooling / licensing: [cost estimate]
Change management: [communication, training effort]

## Progress Tracking
Check-in cadence: [weekly / monthly]
Leading indicator: [early signal the closure is on track]
Lagging indicator: [final evidence the gap is closed]
A common mistake is to assign each maturity dimension to a separate workstream that progresses independently. In practice, dimensions are tightly coupled:
training can be scoped (you need a sanctioned use-case list to teach toward).
can scale beyond pilots (teams cannot be asked to build on an unstable foundation).
instrumentation exists (you cannot measure what you cannot observe).
is not a dimension you close once — it must run in parallel at every horizon as new cohorts of users engage with Claude.

A pragmatic orchestration model: define a 'dimension dependency graph' at programme kick-off, assign a lead for each dimension, hold a weekly cross-dimension sync to surface blockers, and escalate to the executive sponsor when a blocker crosses the 2-week threshold.
Tracking Progression: Leading and Lagging Indicators
Maturity progression plans fail not because of poor design but because of poor visibility. Effective tracking requires both types of indicators:
Predicts future maturity gain; measurable before the gap is closed
% of engineers enrolled in prompt engineering module; # of prompts submitted to shared library
Confirms the gap is closed; measurable after the change is in place
% of production use cases using versioned prompts; reduction in prompt-regression incidents
Signals whether the closure plan itself is on track
% of closure activities completed on schedule; number of open blockers older than 2 weeks
Track leading indicators weekly. Review lagging indicators at each quarterly business review. If a leading indicator stalls for two consecutive weeks, treat it as a blocker, not a lag.
Quarterly Business Reviews for Maturity Progression
The Quarterly Business Review (QBR) is the primary governance mechanism for maturity progression. The agenda should be fixed across quarters to enable comparison:
Maturity score update — re-run the assessment rubric for the two dimensions targeted this quarter and present before/after scores
Gap closure status — red/amber/green on each closure plan; root-cause any red items
Horizon adjustment — confirm H1/H2/H3 sequencing is still valid given what has changed in the business or technology landscape
Resource re-allocation — reallocate budget and headcount from deprioritised gaps to newly urgent ones
Executive decisions required — surface the two to three decisions only an executive sponsor can make (policy exceptions, budget releases, organisational changes)
Common Pitfalls in Gap Closure Programmes
Each dimension appears to progress but integrated delivery stalls
Maintain a shared dependency graph; require cross-dimension sign-off before declaring a gap closed
New platforms deployed but adoption is low
Match tooling investment with change-management investment 1:1; do not deploy tools ahead of the training that enables use
Cannot demonstrate ROI; executive support erodes
Treat measurement capability as a gap in its own right; close it in H1 before the portfolio scales
Technical gaps close but usage does not grow
Assign culture-and-change a dimension lead with a measurable OKR, not just a communications budget
Programme stalls once 'comfortable' maturity is reached
Set L4–L5 targets at programme inception; make strategic-differentiation goals visible from day one
Capability Gap Closure Metrics and OKR Alignment

Linking capability gap closure activities to company-level OKRs is what converts a maturity programme from an IT initiative into a business imperative. Without this link, closures are evaluated on technical completeness rather than business impact — and they lose executive support the moment competing priorities emerge.
The recommended approach is a three-level metric cascade:
40% of customer-service interactions resolved without human escalation by Q4
6 of 8 identified priority gaps closed by end of H2
Prompt-ops pipeline deployed; CI gate failure rate < 2%
Prompt engineering training delivered to 200 users

Each gap closure plan should include a field that maps its target state to at least one company OKR. This forces prioritisation: if a gap closure cannot be traced to any company objective, it is a strong signal to defer it.
Align your maturity programme to revenue, cost, risk, or customer experience OKRs. Maturity for its own sake does not survive budget cycles.
Phased Resourcing Model for Gap Closure

Gap closure programmes often underestimate the resource investment required. Technical tooling costs are visible; the hidden costs are change management, training design, and ongoing governance overhead. The phased resourcing model below provides calibration benchmarks for typical enterprise programmes:

These are not sequential headcounts — H2 and H3 resources are additive. By month 15, a programme at H3 typically has 10–18 FTE in total dedicated or dedicated-equivalent allocation. Programmes that try to run on 2 FTE through all three horizons stall at the H1–H2 transition.
Automation and Tooling for Gap Tracking

Manual spreadsheet tracking of 20–40 gap closure plans across six maturity dimensions is unsustainable beyond month three. Effective programmes implement lightweight tooling to automate progress aggregation:

A programme dashboard built on this data model can render dimension-level RAG status, surface blockers automatically, and compute a consolidated maturity score — replacing the error-prone quarterly manual survey with near-real-time visibility.
Across enterprise Claude programmes, six patterns consistently accelerate gap closure without sacrificing quality:
Use one reference implementation per gap.

Rather than asking every team to close the same gap independently, designate one team to build the reference closure, then enable others to adopt it. This is how the internal developer platform pattern spreads from the CoE outward.
Gate use-case approvals on prerequisite gaps.

Do not approve a new use case in the portfolio intake process if the governance or safety gaps it depends on are not yet at the required level. This creates pull demand for gap closure rather than relying on push from the programme office.
Co-locate gap owners and platform engineers.

Gap closure work that happens in separate silos consistently misses deadlines. Even a weekly two-hour working session where gap owners and platform engineers are in the same room (physical or virtual) dramatically reduces back-and-forth latency.
A half-day internal showcase where teams demonstrate closed gaps to peers both celebrates progress and triggers organic adoption of new capabilities across the organisation.

Horizon 1 has a strong tendency to expand as teams discover adjacent gaps. Enforce a hard 90-day time-box. Anything not closed in H1 that does not meet the 'blocking gate' definition moves to the H2 backlog — it does not extend H1.

Organisations that make current maturity scores visible to all engineering and product leads see 30–50% faster voluntary gap closure compared to those where scores are shared only in executive reviews. Visibility creates peer accountability without administrative overhead.
Capability gap closure plans should be a mandatory input to enterprise architecture (EA) review processes. This integration serves two purposes:

EA reviews that approve new Claude use cases without checking prerequisite maturity gaps create technical and governance debt that compounds over time. A single policy requiring maturity gate confirmation before use-case approval enforces closure discipline across the programme.

Gap closure plans often require infrastructure changes (new API gateways, expanded IAM policies, additional logging pipelines) that have long lead times in governed environments. Integrating gap plans with EA review gives architecture teams the foresight to schedule these changes without blocking programme delivery.

The practical implementation is a one-page 'maturity gate check' appended to every EA review submission, listing which maturity dimensions the proposed use case depends on, the current level of each, and the target level required for safe operation.
Beyond the pitfalls table above, three subtler anti-patterns specifically affect progression plans — not just gap closure in isolation:
A programme that advances rapidly across many dimensions simultaneously, reaching L2 on all six, but never advances any single dimension to L3 or above.

Dimension tourism results from resource allocation that spreads effort evenly rather than prioritising. Each quarterly report shows progress everywhere — but no dimension is deep enough to enable the next tier of use cases.
Set a minimum depth rule: at least two dimensions must reach L4 before a new dimension is added to the active progression plan. Depth first, breadth second.
Maturity scores increase each quarter, but observable business outcomes do not. The gap between self-assessed maturity and actual demonstrated capability widens.
Assessment rubrics are completed by the teams being assessed. Without external validation, scores drift toward what teams believe they deserve rather than what the evidence supports.

Introduce a peer-review mechanism where a team from a different business unit reviews the evidence behind each maturity score before it is finalised. Annual external validation by a third party provides an additional check.
Under delivery pressure, H2 and H3 activities are absorbed into H1, creating an overloaded first phase that delivers nothing on time.
Executives see H2 and H3 capabilities in roadmaps and ask why they cannot be delivered immediately. Without clear horizon definitions, programme managers accommodate the request.
Define horizon gates formally: H2 cannot begin until all H1 'blocking gate' gaps are confirmed closed. Put this in the programme charter and enforce it at the first QBR.
is the operational engine of maturity progression. It transforms assessment findings into sequenced, owned, time-bound plans that the programme can execute quarter over quarter.
Identify blocking gates first — no gap below L2 in governance or safety should be left open while scaling use cases
Sequence gaps across three horizons: foundational readiness, scaled adoption, and strategic differentiation
Prioritise by impact and effort — address priority gaps (high impact, achievable) before strategic bets (high impact, long horizon)
Maintain a dependency graph across dimensions — cross-dimension coupling is the most common source of programme stalls
Track leading indicators weekly and lagging indicators per quarter — never let a stalled metric go unaddressed for more than two check-ins
Use the Quarterly Business Review as the standing forum for maturity score updates, resource reallocation, and executive decisions

Organisations that maintain this rhythm — assess, plan, close, measure, review — consistently reach L4 maturity within 18 months and build the compounding advantages that define long-term competitive differentiation with Claude.
